region_of_origin: Guangdong, China; widely consumed in Singapore and Southeast Asia

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term specific to lao po bing or Chinese wintermelon pastry exists; 'pastry food product' (FOODON_03301116) used as the closest defensible broad match.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Pastries and cakes) is the closest defensible category; no specific code for Chinese flaky pastry with wintermelon filling exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for lao po bing or Chinese wife's pastry identified in the clinical terminology; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard lao po bing piece weighs approximately 55–65 g; a single piece (60 g) provides about 256 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Lao Po Bing (Wife's Pastry) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Lao po bing (wife's pastry), CR ID F11110016, with confidence 0.95. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are sourced from this entry.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of baked pastry / sweet biscuit analogues — University of Sydney GI Database; supplemented by Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for lao po bing exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 70 (high) based on the closest analogous foods: sweet biscuits and shortcrust pastry items with refined wheat flour and high sugar content, which cluster at GI 65–75 in the Sydney database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international tables. Carbohydrate per serving (60 g piece) = 53.45 × 0.60 = 32.07 g; GL = 70 × 32.07 / 100 = 22.4. Estimation is conservative given the very low dietary fibre (0.86 g/100g) and high refined sugar (16.98 g/100g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (16.98 g/100g) with very low fibre (0.86 g/100g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; saturated fat (~8.5 g/100g) contributes to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Despite a moderate sodium level of 114 mg/100g, individuals with hypertension should limit lao po bing to occasional small portions (30–40 g) because the high refined sugar and saturated fat content independently elevate blood pressure risk through insulin-mediated sodium retention and vascular stiffness per AHA 2018 guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate 53.45 g/100g with fibre only 0.86 g/100g and sugar 16.98 g/100g; estimated GI 70 (high) yields GL ~22 per standard serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lao po bing is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high refined carbohydrate load (53.45 g/100g), very low dietary fibre (0.86 g/100g), and high sugar content (16.98 g/100g), which together produce a rapid and significant postprandial blood glucose spike; if consumed at all, portions should be restricted to 20–30 g on rare occasions with close blood glucose monitoring.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat ~8.5 g/100g from lard or shortening, trace trans fat ~0.2 g/100g, and high refined sugar 16.98 g/100g collectively elevate LDL cholesterol and cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit lao po bing because its high saturated fat content (~8.5 g/100g), trace trans fat (~0.2 g/100g from shortening), and high refined sugar (16.98 g/100g) collectively raise LDL cholesterol, promote endothelial dysfunction, and increase atherogenic risk; a single 60 g piece delivers approximately 5.1 g saturated fat, exceeding one-quarter of the AHA daily saturated fat limit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat ~8.5 g/100g exceeds the 5 g/100g RA threshold; trace trans fat ~0.2 g/100g is strongly pro-inflammatory; refined sugar 16.98 g/100g promotes inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Lao po bing is not recommended for individuals managing rheumatoid arthritis because its high saturated fat (~8.5 g/100g from lard or shortening), trace trans fat (~0.2 g/100g), and high refined sugar (16.98 g/100g) collectively promote systemic inflammation; no anti-inflammatory nutrients are present to offset these effects.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lao po bing (老婆饼), literally 'wife's pastry' or 'wife cake', is a traditional Cantonese baked pastry originating from Guangdong, China, and widely popular in Singapore, Hong Kong, and across Southeast Asia. The pastry consists of a thin, multi-layered flaky shell made from laminated wheat flour dough enriched with lard or vegetable shortening, encasing a sweet, dense filling of candied wintermelon paste (dong gua tang) blended with sugar, sesame seeds, and sometimes coconut or almond. The outer surface is typically glazed with egg wash and sprinkled with sesame seeds before baking, producing a golden, slightly crisp exterior with a soft, chewy, intensely sweet interior. Nutritionally, lao po bing is energy-dense at 426 kcal per 100 g, with a high fat content of 21.54 g (predominantly saturated fat ~8.5 g from lard or shortening), high refined carbohydrates at 53.45 g, and high sugar at 16.98 g, while dietary fibre is very low at 0.86 g. Sodium is moderate at 114 mg/100g. The estimated glycemic index is 70 (high), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 22 per standard 60 g serving. Clinically, this pastry is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high refined sugar, saturated fat, and trace trans fat content. It is best consumed as an occasional treat in small portions. In Singapore, lao po bing is sold in traditional Chinese bakeries and is a popular festive and everyday snack.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F11110016) with confidence 0.95 and reviewed status. Saturated fat estimated from typical lao po bing composition (lard/shortening primary fat) as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown; trans fat estimated at trace level from shortening. GI estimated from analogous baked pastry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Overall confidence is high for macros and moderate for fatty acid sub-fractions.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (8.5 g/100g) is estimated based on typical lao po bing formulation using lard or vegetable shortening as the primary fat; HPB Foodo does not report saturated fat separately for this entry. Trans fat (0.2 g/100g) is a trace estimate from shortening use. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not available in HPB Foodo database for this entry. 2. potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for lao po bing (F11110016) as of April 2026. 3. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on sweet biscuit and shortcrust pastry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international tables; no direct GI measurement for lao po bing has been published. 4. Halal status is uncertain as traditional recipes use lard; many commercial versions substitute vegetable shortening and may carry halal certification — consumers should verify with the specific producer. 5. All core macros (protein_g 4.63, fat_g 21.54, carbohydrate_g 53.45) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification is required.
